children.text: Eightieth session – Agenda item 152 – Financing of the United Nations Organization Stabilization Mission in the Democratic Republic of the Congo – Budget performance for the period from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025 and proposed budget for the period from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027 for the United Nations Organization Stabilization Mission in the Democratic Republic of the Congo – Report of the Advisory Committee on Administrative and Budgetary Questions

table_content.text: Proposal submitted by the Secretary-General for 2026/27
Adjustment recommended by the Advisory Committee for 2026/27
Recommendation of the Advisory Committee for 2026/27

a	Estimates as at 28 February 2026.
	b	Excludes one-time requirements for termination entitlements payable to staff members.

a	Estimates as at 28 February 2026.
	b	Excludes one-time requirements for termination entitlements payable to staff members.

children.text: 1.	During its consideration of the report on the financing of the United Nations Organization Stabilization Mission in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (MONUSCO) (A/80/624), the Advisory Committee on Administrative and Budgetary Questions met with representatives of the Secretary-General, who provided additional information and clarification, concluding with written responses received on 8 April 2026. The comments and recommendations of the Committee on cross-cutting issues related to peacekeeping operations are contained in its report (A/80/604), and those pertaining to the findings and recommendations of the Board of Auditors on United Nations peacekeeping operations for the period from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025 can be found in its related report (A/80/633).

text: II.	Budget performance report for the period from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025

children.text: 2.	The General Assembly, by its resolution 78/301, appropriated an amount of $907,983,500 gross ($883,923,700 net) for the maintenance of the Mission for the period from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025. Expenditure for the period totalled $893,099,400 gross ($863,212,600 net) for a budget implementation rate of 98.4 per cent, reflecting primarily: (a) higher actual vacancy rates for uniformed personnel; (b) a lower number of hours flown; and (c) delays in the implementation of programmatic activities; partly offset by the payment of evacuation allowances and an increase in the salary scale for national staff.

3.	The Advisory Committee notes from the information provided to it that there were significant redeployments in the performance period due to the security situation in North Kivu from January 2025 and the evacuation of staff from Goma. Upon enquiry, the Committee was informed that the costs for the evacuation of personnel from Goma in the 2024/25 period amounted to $21,332,437 (or 2.4 per cent of the appropriation). The Committee was provided with the below table containing the details of the evacuation costs.
Evacuation costs – (Thousands of United States dollars)

table_content.text: Entitlement travel/relocation and daily subsistence allowance
Entitlement travel/relocation and daily subsistence allowance
Security evacuation allowance for staff and dependants
National Professional Officers and General Service staff
Daily subsistence allowance for relocation of staff and dependants
Daily subsistence allowance for United Nations Volunteers and dependants
Daily subsistence allowance for United Nations Volunteers and dependants
Daily subsistence allowance for government-provided personnel
Daily subsistence allowance for staff who remained at their duty station during the crisis
Hotel accommodation for staff and dependants
Repairs, maintenance and utilities at Entebbe International Airport
Shuttle buses and transportation costs for staff and dependants
Relocation of United Nations personnel luggage and goods (cargo), daily subsistence allowance for drivers, fuel
Flight, aircrew accommodation, ground handling of aircraft at Entebbe International Airport
Ration packs and meals for staff and dependants while in transit

children.text: 4.	As indicated in section V.B of the report of the Secretary-General, the overall material loss attributable to the Alliance Fleuve Congo/Mouvement du 23 mars incursion in Goma in January 2025 is assessed at $4.17 million, of which $1.6 million, or 39 per cent, is the subject of an insurance claim that is still under negotiation. The Advisory Committee trusts that detailed information on any recovery from the insurance claim will be provided to the General Assembly in the next budget report.

5.	The Advisory Committee notes that expenditures in the 2024/25 period were significantly affected by the security situation in the area of operation and by restrictions on movement and on the use of the Goma airport, as well as by additional expenses related to evacuation. The Committee trusts that more detailed information on redeployments will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report, in particular with a breakdown between lower-than-budgeted expenditures related to security and movement constraints, and to any activities curtailed by liquidity challenges.

6.	The Advisory Committee notes from information provided to it that the unliquidated obligations amounted to $100,307,500 (or 11.0 per cent of the appropriation) as at 30 June 2025, reflecting a decrease of $33,677,900 compared with the amount of $133,985,400 as at 30 June 2024.

7.	The unencumbered balance of $14,884,100 in gross terms represents 1.6 per cent of the overall level of resources approved for the financial period. A detailed analysis of variances is provided in section III of the report of the Secretary-General. Information provided to the Advisory Committee indicates that other revenue and adjustments for the 2024/25 period amounted to $30,420,100, comprising: (a) investment revenue ($9,558,100); (b) other/miscellaneous revenue ($2,543,000), including other revenue ($1,145,800) and the refund of prior-period expenses ($1,397,200); and (c) the cancellation of prior-period obligations ($18,319,000). The Committee notes that the report of the Secretary-General, in paragraphs 82 and 137, proposes that credits arising from the above-mentioned revenue and adjustments be placed in a reserve that would be used in the event that collections are insufficient to enable the full, efficient and effective implementation of mandates (see para. 63 below).

8.	The Advisory Committee notes that, according to section II.E of the report, the level of the cancellation of prior-period obligations declined by $739,600 from the 2023/24 amount of $19,058,600. However, the amount resulting from the cancellation of prior-period obligations still represents 2.0 per cent of the approved budget for the 2024/25 period.
Matters pertaining to the report of the Board of Auditors

9.	In considering the report of the Secretary-General on the financing of MONUSCO, the Advisory Committee also had before it the report of the Board of Auditors on the accounts of the United Nations peacekeeping operations for the financial period ended 30 June 2025. In its report, the Board noted the ongoing challenges with liquidity and deficiencies in the disarmament, demobilization and reintegration processes followed by the Mission (A/80/5 (Vol. II), chap. II, paras. 177–199 and 304–409). The Advisory Committee trusts that the recommendations of the Board of Auditors will be implemented in a timely manner.

text: III.	Information on performance for the current period

children.text: 10.	Regarding current and projected expenditure for the period from 1 July 2025 to 30 June 2026, the Advisory Committee was informed upon enquiry that, as at 28 February 2026, expenditure amounted to $479,290,669 (gross). At the end of the current period, the estimated total expenditures would amount to $738,644,100, with a projected unencumbered balance of $130,483,100, or 15.0 per cent. As at 4 March 2026, the allotment received by the Mission for 2025/26 was $579,950,000.

11.	Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that the contingency plan of MONUSCO significantly curtailed the Mission’s scope and flexibility, with uneven effects across areas and sectors. Fewer personnel and assets led to reduced patrols, base closures and limits on intelligence, surveillance and rapid response, although core protection at internally displaced persons sites was largely maintained. The Committee was further informed that adjustments in North Kivu stemmed from both funding limitations and the volatile security situation, leading to varied effects across regions. In Petit Nord, Alliance Fleuve Congo/Mouvement du 23 mars activity and access restrictions forced MONUSCO to scale back operations – repatriating over 1,100 troops, closing two Goma-area bases and adopting a more mobile posture while maintaining key deployments near population centres. In contrast, adjustments in the Beni territory of Grand Nord were driven mainly by liquidity constraints, focused on optimizing deployments, such as relocating 182 personnel to support offensive operations against armed groups and moving force headquarters to Beni. This allowed continued protection and stabilization efforts with minimal disruption. Similarly, in Ituri, adaptations prioritized resource efficiency rather than security concerns.

12.	The Advisory Committee notes that, in implementing the contingency plan, MONUSCO has prioritized core, life-saving tasks while scaling back or deferring lower-impact activities. The Committee also notes that the diminished presence in some regions reflected both liquidity constraints and severe operational constraints experienced by the Mission. The Committee trusts that an update and a more detailed assessment and breakdown distinguishing the impact of the contingency plan and of the operational constraints on the implementation of the mandate of MONUSCO will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report and in the next performance report. The Committee also trusts that all efforts will be undertaken to ensure the security and safety of personnel.

13.	The Advisory Committee was informed that, as at 13 February 2026, a total of $27,623,080,000 had been assessed on Member States in respect of MONUSCO since its inception. Payments received as at the same date amounted to $27,174,789,100, resulting in an outstanding balance of $448,290,900. The Committee was further informed that, as at 16 February 2026, the cash available to the Mission amounted to $316,976,379, which is sufficient to cover the three-month operating reserve of $144,687,925 (excluding reimbursements to troop- and police-contributing countries).

14.	According to the supplementary information provided to the Advisory Committee, as at 31 December 2025, claims for the reimbursement of troop costs had been settled up to September 2025, leaving an outstanding balance of $35,389,800. Contingent-owned equipment had been certified up to September 2025 and paid up to December 2024, leaving an outstanding balance of $111,360,300 as at 31 December 2025. With regard to death and disability compensation, as at 18 February 2026, an amount of $11,835,600 had been paid to settle 461 death and disability claims since the inception of MONUSCO, with 25 claims pending. The Advisory Committee trusts that all outstanding liabilities and claims will be settled in a timely manner.

text: IV.	Proposed budget for the period from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027

children.text: A.	Mandate and planning assumptions

15.	The mandate of MONUSCO was established by the Security Council in its resolution 1925 (2010) and most recently extended by the Council until 20 December 2026 in its resolution 2808 (2025). By the same resolution, the Mission has been authorized to support the implementation of a permanent ceasefire in line with resolution 2773 (2025). Additional resources are being sought for this activity (A/80/624, para. 22). The planning assumptions and mission support initiatives of the Mission for the 2026/27 period are summarized in section I.C of the report of the Secretary-General on the proposed budget for the Mission (A/80/624).
Ceasefire implementation

16.	Under Security Council resolution 2808 (2025), MONUSCO is authorized to support the implementation of a permanent ceasefire, including through participation in the Ceasefire Oversight and Verification Mechanism and the provision of technical and logistical assistance to the Expanded Joint Verification Mechanism Plus. According to the report, this support focuses strictly on ceasefire-related tasks, excluding protection-of-civilians or State-authority functions, and will be phased and scaled to the level of participation agreed by the parties and endorsed by the Security Council (ibid., paras. 47–51).

17.	The Advisory Committee notes that paragraph 53 of Security Council resolution 2808 (2025) requested the Secretary-General to report to the Council by 1 March 2026 with progress towards the establishment and operationalization of the Ceasefire Oversight and Verification Mechanism, as well as realistic, concrete, cost effective and operational proposals on how MONUSCO could further support the implementation of a permanent ceasefire, especially in the hotspot areas of North and South Kivu, while also indicating that the Council intends to consider, in a later resolution and taking into account proposals by the Secretary-General and the evolving situation on the ground, any necessary adjustments to the mandate of MONUSCO. Upon request, the Committee was provided with a copy of the Secretary-General’s letter dated 27 February 2026 addressed to the President of the Security Council (S/2026/105). The letter lists a set of conditions required for the effective operation of United Nations peacekeepers in North and South Kivu, including full freedom of movement in all areas; unimpeded access to Goma and Kavumu airports, relevant landing sites and any future United Nations installations; non-use of offensive drone capabilities; and uninterrupted fuel supply lines. According to the letter, over a progressive deployment phase of up to 180 days, the Mission would deploy mobile and static teams of military observers and liaison officers, establish operating bases to secure those teams, assist incident management and support de-escalation in close coordination with the parties and stakeholders.

18.	Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that the Mission’s ceasefire support model relies on existing capacities for initial implementation. Practically, MONUSCO can begin with liaison, coordination and limited monitoring, later expanding to broader field deployments and enhanced verification once conditions and resources allow.

19.	The Advisory Committee notes the conditions listed by the Secretary-General for MONUSCO to play an effective role in support of the implementation of a permanent ceasefire and trusts that detailed updates on the attainment of such conditions and their impact on mandate operationalization will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report.
Impact of the UN80 Initiative and culture of efficiency

20.	Information on efficiency gains is provided in section II.C of the report of the Secretary-General. Estimated efficiencies for 2026/27 represent a 9.2 per cent reduction compared to the approved budget for 2025/26, in the context of an overall 0.7 per cent proposed increase (see para. 22 below). Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that the 2026/27 efficiency plan of MONUSCO streamlines functions to cut costs while safeguarding operational capacity through optimized reporting lines, merged complementary roles and the redeployment of non-location-dependent functions to lower-cost Entebbe, Uganda.

21.	The Advisory Committee notes that, while some efficiencies presented result from process improvements, others are driven by operational restrictions or by liquidity constraints and temporary expenditure compression. The Committee trusts that further information on the connection between both the indicated efficiencies and the additional requested resources in connection with the support to the ceasefire implementation and changes in the expected accomplishments and outputs proposed for MONUSCO for 2026/27 will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report, to allow for a better assessment of the sustainability and long-term implications of such measures on the implementation of the Mission’s mandate. The Committee will further discuss efficiency measures in its report on cross-cutting issues related to peacekeeping operations (A/80/604).
B.	Resource requirements
Financial resources – (Thousands of United States dollars)

22.	The proposed requirements for 2026/27 amount to $874,790,100 and represent an increase of $5,662,900, or 0.7 per cent, compared with the provision of $869,127,200 approved for the 2025/26 period. Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that the proposed $5.7 million budget increase for 2026/27 is tied to the expanded mandate of MONUSCO under Security Council resolution 2808 (2025).
Proposed resources for ceasefire implementation

23.	Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that the proposed resource requirements for the military and support components of MONUSCO include personnel increases for units such as rapid deployment, infantry and light infantry battalions, the aeromedical evacuation team, unmanned aircraft system operators, signals platoons and the Geolocation and Threat Assessment Unit. Facility and infrastructure funding covers $4.2 million for critical maintenance and $11.6 million for the construction of new ceasefire monitoring camps. An additional $2.8 million is allocated to fuel for operating these facilities. Communications and information technology resources include $1.2 million for new hardware and connectivity equipment for field offices, $5 million for intelligence, surveillance and reconnaissance assets and counter unmanned aircraft systems, $1.7 million for service subscriptions and $10 million to establish a signal intelligence capability. The budget includes $1.1 million for ground transportation to acquire 20 vehicles, rent buses for troop movements to ceasefire related sites and cover fuel and maintenance costs. The proposed resources for the new mandate are provided in the table below.
Ceasefire implementation requirements – (Thousands of United States dollars)

table_content.text: 4 Professional and 3 Field Service positions (redistribution of existing capacity)
Travel within and outside Mission area
1 unmanned aircraft system class III and 3 unmanned aircraft systems class II
Satellite services and information technology equipment

children.text: 24.	The Advisory Committee notes the listed conditions presented by the Secretary-General for the deployment of troops in support of the implementation of a permanent ceasefire (see para. 19 above). While acknowledging the need for flexibility and preparedness to support the new mandate, the Committee trusts that the Mission will adopt a phased approach to the planned expenditures, reflective of the security situation and cooperation of the parties to the conflict. The Committee makes further comments and recommendations on the proposed resources in paragraphs 44 to 55 below.
1.	Military and police personnel
a	Represents the highest level of authorized strength.
Delayed deployment factors – (Percentage)

25.	The proposed resources for military and police personnel for 2026/27 reflect a decrease of $28,798,500, or 7.0 per cent, compared with the apportionment for 2025/26. The proposed decrease is mainly attributable to: (a) to the application of the higher vacancy rate for military observers, formed police units and United Nations police personnel; and (b) reduced requirements for military contingent personnel, reflecting the net impact of efficiency measures; offset in part by increased requirements for incoming contingent personnel to support the new ceasefire implementation mandate (A/80/624, paras. 93, 96, 98 and 101).
26.	Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was provided with the table below containing a detailed breakdown of military and police personnel in the current period and planned for 2026/27.
Military and police deployment
a	Includes ceasefire requirements.
b	Based on the deployment plan for 2026/27 and subject to operational requirements and liquidity situations.

27.	Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that, as part of its efficiency measures amid force reductions, MONUSCO has shifted to a readiness-based model that relies on mobile quick-reaction forces and targeted operations rather than maintaining a permanent presence in all locations.

28.	With regard to the new mandate, the Secretary-General’s letter to the Security Council (see para. 19 above) indicates that, at present, MONUSCO has deployed 8,131 of its authorized 11,500 military personnel and is using existing resources to support early ceasefire implementation. MONUSCO could potentially need 1,500–2,500 additional troops within its current troop ceiling to provide sustained ceasefire-monitoring coverage across North and South Kivu. Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that the deployment of additional MONUSCO troops to support ceasefire implementation will depend on conditions on the ground and progress achieved by the Expanded Joint Verification Mechanism Plus. Information provided to the Committee upon enquiry indicates that 2,498 is the military contingent planned for 12 months in support of the ceasefire implementation mandate but that, for the period from July to October 2026, no funding is sought for the related uniformed personnel, corresponding to the four-month delay indicated in the report (A/80/624, para. 84, table).

29.	The Advisory Committee notes that the number of troops indicated as planned after the contingency plan is higher than the actual number of troops deployed in January 2026. The Committee also notes that the application of the vacancy rate of 9.9 per cent to military contingent personnel corresponds to a planned average deployment of 10,360 military personnel for the 12 months of the 2026/27 period. Considering the indicated funding of 2,498 military contingent personnel for eight months for ceasefire monitoring, or an average yearly deployment of 1,666 military personnel, the budget also estimates an average additional deployment of 560 troops when compared to the actual additional deployment indicated in the Secretary-General’s letter. The Advisory Committee trusts that further information will be provided to the General Assembly with regard to the difference in the planned additional military contingent personnel which is not related to the ceasefire implementation, with further details on its relation to the reversibility of contingency measures and constraints in the area of operations.

30.	The Advisory Committee further notes that the Secretary-General’s letter to the Security Council refers to a potential need for 1,500–2,500 additional troops within the existing troop ceiling and a progressive deployment phase of up to 180 days. The Committee trusts that updated information on the planned deployment of troops related to ceasefire implementation and on how the latest plan corresponds to proposed funding will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report.
31.	The Advisory Committee recommends the approval of the proposals of the Secretary-General for military and police personnel.
2.	Civilian personnel

32.	The proposed resources for civilian personnel for 2026/27 reflect an increase of $1,028,600, or 0.4 per cent, compared with the apportionment for 2025/26, mainly due to higher requirements under the international staff, National Professional Officer and national General Service staff categories, partially offset by lower requirements for United Nations Volunteers and general temporary assistance positions. The report further indicates that the increases are driven primarily by the following: (a) the introduction of danger pay for Goma, as of 1 April 2025; (b) the application of a lower vacancy rate; and (c) the application of a higher common staff cost rate for international staff positions based in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (ibid., paras. 103, 106, 109, 112 and 114).

33.	Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that the higher percentage applied to international staff salaries in calculating common staff costs for the Democratic Republic of the Congo includes evacuation-related expenses, particularly evacuation travel. Excluding these costs would lower the rate from 90.1 per cent to 85.8 per cent, reducing proposed resources by $2.2 million. The Advisory Committee considers that the common staff cost methodology should be refined to ensure that exceptional, one-time costs are not included as part of underlying cost drivers of future expenditure assumptions for improved budget accuracy. Taking into consideration the exceptional circumstances that resulted in the evacuation of staff from Goma in the 2024/25 period, the Committee recommends that the rate for common staff costs be adjusted downward from 90.1 per cent to 85.8 per cent, resulting in a reduction of the provision for international staff by $2,200,000 (see also A/76/7, para. 24, and A/78/7/Add.42, para. 16). The Committee further discusses the common staff costs methodology in its report on cross-cutting issues related to peacekeeping operations (A/80/604).
Recommendations on posts and positions

34.	The Secretary-General proposes a total of 2,187 civilian posts and positions for the 2026/27 period, reflecting the abolishment of 150 posts and positions (1 D-1, 1 P-5, 1 P-4, 2 P-3, 9 Field Service, 6 National Professional Officers, 105 national General Service, 7 United Nations Volunteers and 18 general temporary assistance national General Service). The Secretary-General also proposes the nationalization of 42 international posts (2 P-4, 7 P-3, 6 P-2, 10 Field Service and 17 international United Nations Volunteers) to 15 National Professional Officers, 10 national General Service and 17 national United Nations Volunteers. The report also indicates the proposed downward reclassification of five positions (1 P-5 and 4 P-4), the functional reassignment of four positions (1 P-5, 1 P-3 and 2 Field Service), the redeployment of 119 posts from the Mission’s duty stations in the Democratic Republic of the Congo to Entebbe, the redeployment of a further 12 posts from Entebbe to duty stations in the Democratic Republic of the Congo and the proposed redeployment of one Head of Political Affairs (D-1) from the Office of the Deputy Special Representative of the Secretary-General/Resident Coordinator/Humanitarian Coordinator to the Office of the Special Representative of the Secretary-General. The list of posts and positions proposed for continuation, abolishment, conversion, reassignment or reclassification are presented in table 2 of the report (ibid., paras. 67 to 73).

35.	Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that the proposed 2026/27 civilian staffing reductions are guided by the 2024 civilian staffing review and subsequent operational and financial developments, aimed at streamlining functions, reducing duplication and aligning resources with the transition priorities of MONUSCO. The plan reflects a strategic reconfiguration of the Mission’s structure: downsizing in Goma, reducing non-essential roles in Kinshasa, reinforcing staff in Beni and Bunia and expanding the role of Entebbe as a logistics hub. The Committee was further informed that the shifting of staff to Entebbe, which is part of the MONUSCO area of operations, is considered permanent, as it enhances cost efficiency by relocating functions to a lower-cost duty station and introduces a new operational model that minimizes presence in high-risk zones and supports enhanced mandate delivery. The Advisory Committee trusts that more detailed information on functions proposed to be redeployed to Entebbe and the criteria applied by the Mission in that regard will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report.
Vacant posts

36.	Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that, as at 28 February 2026, there were 711 vacant posts and positions. According to supplementary information provided to the Committee, as at 31 December 2025, MONUSCO had 63 posts vacant for more than two years, of which 25 are proposed for abolishment in 2026/27, 2 for redeployment and the rest for retention. The Committee was also informed upon enquiry that, of the 150 posts and positions proposed for abolition, 130 are vacant and 20 will be vacated on 30 June 2026, either through the non-renewal of contracts or payment of the corresponding termination indemnities, should the proposed positions be abolished. The Advisory Committee trusts that an update on the number of vacant posts and their recruitment status will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report and in the next budget submission. The Committee also trusts that remaining long-vacant posts will continue to be critically reviewed in the context of the next budget submission. The Committee discusses vacant and long-vacant posts in its report on cross-cutting issues related to peacekeeping operations (A/80/604).

37.	Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that 54 positions (15 international and 39 national) encumbered by staff with approved requests for early separation remained as at 31 December 2025. The Advisory Committee trusts that detailed and updated information on all staff of the Mission approved for the early separation programme, as well as on the related costs, will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report and in the next budget submission.
Vacancy rates
Vacancy rates – (Percentage)

38.	The Advisory Committee trusts that the Secretary-General will provide updated information on the latest actual and actual average vacancy rates to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report, along with clear justification where the proposed rates differ from the actual rates, including as it relates to the impact of proposed abolishments, realistic projected recruitment and potential upcoming vacancies, to the extent possible.
Nationalization of posts and positions

39.	Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that the percentage of national staff approved for 2025/26 was 61 per cent and that, under the proposed estimate for 2026/27, the percentage of national staff would increase to 62 per cent. In the periods from 2022/23 to 2026/27, the Mission has proposed 78 positions for nationalization, as detailed in the table below:
Positions proposed for nationalization

40.	The Advisory Committee acknowledges the proposed nationalization of 42 posts in the 2026/27 period. The Committee encourages continued efforts in this regard, to be presented in future budget submissions (see also A/78/744/Add.9, para. 36, and A/77/767/Add.8, para. 23).
41.	Subject to its recommendation in paragraph 33 above, the Advisory Committee recommends the approval of the proposals of the Secretary-General for civilian personnel.
3.	Operational costs – (Thousands of United States dollars)

42.	The proposed resources for operational costs for the 2026/27 period represent a net increase of $33,432,800, or 15.4 per cent, compared with the resources approved for the 2025/26 period. The proposed net increase for 2026/27 reflects higher requirements for facilities and infrastructure, air operations, communications and information technology, ground transportation, official travel and consultants and consultancy services, offset in part by proposed decreases under other object classes (see also A/80/624, sects. II and III). The Advisory Committee trusts that updated information on the planned deployment for the ceasefire implementation mandate, and any related impact on corresponding planned expenditures under operational costs, will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report.
Consultants and consulting services

43.	The proposed resources for consultants and consulting services amount to $600,900 for 2026/27, representing an increase of $156,100, or 35.1 per cent, compared with the approved level of $444,800 for 2025/26. The Advisory Committee notes expenditures of $173,100 and $492,000 against the appropriations of $796,700 and $516,900 in the 2024/25 and 2023/24 periods, respectively. The Committee notes that no resources under consultants and consulting services are proposed in relation to the ceasefire implementation mandate. Taking into consideration the recent pattern of underexpenditure, the Advisory Committee recommends a reduction of 10 per cent, or $60,100, for resources under consultants and consulting services.
Facilities and infrastructure

44.	The proposed resources for facilities and infrastructure amount to $67,846,200 for 2026/27, representing an increase of $21,028,900, or 44.9 per cent, compared with the approved level of $46,817,300 for 2025/26. The Advisory Committee notes expenditures of $47,450,600 and $57,375,900 against appropriations of $45,502,500 and $49,573,100 in the 2024/25 and 2023/24 periods, respectively. The Committee also notes that the cancellation of prior-period obligations under facilities and infrastructure totalled $1,416,600 during the 2024/25 period and $2,465,400 during the 2023/24 period. The Committee further notes that, excluding resources to sustain and expand its support of ceasefire implementation, in a total amount of $18.6 million (see the third table above), proposed resources still represent an increase in comparison to the appropriation for 2025/26. The Advisory Committee trusts that more information on efficiencies achieved under facilities and infrastructure as a result of the reductions in the level of operations, excluding the ceasefire implementation mandate, will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report.

45.	According to the information provided to the Advisory Committee, MONUSCO is planning six major construction projects for the 2026/27 period, lasting one year and totalling $21.7 million. This includes $4.2 million for maintaining four existing camps through essential services; $11.6 million for constructing new prefabricated camps to host additional monitoring teams; and $2.8 million for fuel costs for operations and maintenance. The Committee notes that $11.6 million for new camps is the amount of planned construction in South Kivu. The Advisory Committee is of the view that the operational restrictions experienced by the Mission in the area of operations may cause delays in the implementation of major construction projects. The Committee also notes the level of cancellation of prior-period obligations in previous years. In view of the above, the Committee recommends that a reduction of 5 per cent, or $3,392,300, be applied for resources under facilities and infrastructure.
Ground transportation

46.	The proposed resources for ground transportation amount to $9,635,400 for 2026/27, representing an increase of $1,513,300, or 18.6 per cent, compared with the approved level of $8,122,100 for 2025/26. The Advisory Committee notes expenditures of $6,691,900 and $10,045,400 against appropriations of $8,019,600 and $8,582,300 in the 2024/25 and 2023/24 periods, respectively. For 2026/27, the report further indicates that the increased requirements mainly reflect higher costs for spare parts needed to repair and upgrade strategic transport assets in support of the new mandate.

47.	Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that the $1.1 million related to the ceasefire implementation mandate would cover deployment and sustainment transport costs, as well as acquisition costs for right-hand-drive vehicles for Entebbe. MONUSCO reviewed its vehicle fleet based on age and mileage, exploring internal redeployment options; the review found redeployment unfeasible in Entebbe due to vehicle age and right-hand-drive models, and air transfer from Kinshasa too costly. Therefore, the purchase of new right-hand-drive light passenger vehicles in Entebbe is justified to support the ceasefire mandate. In the eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo, vehicles meeting write-off criteria will be disposed of without replacement because of reduced staff and surplus capacity. The Committee notes that the appropriated amount for the acquisition of vehicles in 2023/24 was $95,300 and expenditure totalled $3,160,600. In the period 2024/25, no appropriation was made under acquisition of vehicles, but expenditure totalled $463,400.

48.	The Advisory Committee trusts that further clarification on the proposed acquisition of vehicles in Entebbe and the new ceasefire implementation mandate will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report. The Committee also notes the amount spent on the unplanned acquisition of vehicles in recent periods and trusts that further replacement will be done in a phased manner. Therefore, the Committee recommends a proposed reduction 3 per cent, or $289,100, for resources under ground transportation.
Air operations

49.	The proposed resources for air operations amount to $92,722,300 for 2026/27, representing an increase of $10,933,200, or 13.4 per cent, compared with the approved level of $81,789,100 for 2025/26. The Advisory Committee notes expenditures of $57,001,500 and $66,970,500 against appropriations of $97,034,900 and $115,684,700 in the 2024/25 and 2023/24 periods, respectively. The Committee further notes that the cancellation of prior-period obligations totalled $1,952,400 during the 2024/25 period and $2,497,300 during the 2023/24 period.

50.	The Advisory Committee notes that the methodology for calculating flight hours has not been applied in the preparation of the budget for 2026/27. Upon enquiry, in noting that no resources under air operations other than unmanned aircraft system-related requirements have been proposed in relation to the ceasefire mandate (see the third table above), the Committee was informed that the Mission expected that flight hours support needed for ceasefire implementation will be within the budgeted 2026/27 flight hours; therefore, no incremental flight hours have been included. The Advisory Committee trusts that further information on planning assumptions for the calculation of flight hours for the Mission, including estimated support needed for the ceasefire implementation mandate, as well as updates on the level of access to all airports in the area of operations of the Mission, will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report.

51.	Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that proposed requirements for unmanned aircraft systems include one unmanned aircraft system class III, for an amount of $10.8 million for a 12-month period, and three unmanned aircraft systems class II, for an amount of $5 million per unit for a 12-month period. The deployment timeline for the Mission’s new unmanned aircraft systems depends on sourcing – either through troop-contributing countries, allowing deployment within four months under a letter of assist, or through commercial procurement, which typically takes about nine months. The Committee was further informed that the immediate role of the unmanned aircraft systems is safeguarding United Nations personnel and aviation assets during the fragile initial deployment phase, after which – pending consultation with the Ceasefire Oversight and Verification Mechanism – they will support ceasefire monitoring by maintaining situational awareness, detecting and documenting violations and providing imagery for verification. Unmanned aircraft systems are expected to become prominent in the airspace beyond Goma, though coordination with other actors remains uncertain and rules of engagement will be clarified in upcoming Expanded Joint Verification Mechanism Plus technical meetings.

52.	Taking into consideration the extended timelines for sourcing unmanned aircraft systems, as well as the necessary coordination with actors on the ground on their rules of engagement, the Advisory Committee is of the view that the full deployment of the four new unmanned aircraft systems by July is unlikely to proceed as planned, and recommends that a reduction of 10 per cent, or $1,500,000, be applied for resources for unmanned aircraft systems under air operations. The Committee trusts that updated information on the planned deployment timelines will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report.
Communications and information technology

53.	The proposed resources for communications and information technology amount to $43,929,200 for 2026/27, representing an increase of $7,953,100, or 22.1 per cent, compared with the approved level of $35,976,100 for 2025/26. The Advisory Committee notes expenditures of $38,408,200 and $44,004,800 against appropriations of $37,813,900 and $41,022,700 in the 2024/25 and 2023/24 periods, respectively. Upon enquiry, the Committee was informed that $17.9 million in information and communications technology requirements supports the ceasefire mandate and consists of: $1.19 million for the procurement of information and communications technology hardware for the South Kivu field offices and temporary operating bases; $5.0 million for intelligence, surveillance and reconnaissance equipment, including long-range cameras, counter-unmanned aircraft systems, managed services for existing unmanned aircraft system assets and satellite imagery acquisition; $1.7 million for service and maintenance subscriptions; and $10.0 million for the establishment of a signal intelligence capability similar to those of the Geolocation and Threat Assessment Unit.

54.	The Advisory Committee was also informed that resources requested under communications and information technology correspond to supporting the operationalization of existing unmanned aircraft systems, for systems received from the United Nations Multidimensional Integrated Stabilization Mission in Mali. Their use is intended only as an interim solution while MONUSCO awaits the full air operations capabilities for its unmanned aircraft systems. The two United Nations Multidimensional Integrated Stabilization Mission in Mali unmanned aircraft systems class II, which can operate approximately one hour outbound and one hour inbound, will help bridge a critical operational gap and strengthen the Mission’s currently limited unmanned aircraft system assets. Operationalization can be achieved within four months once funding becomes available.

55.	Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that future capability plans similar to those of the Geolocation and Threat Assessment Unit envision relocating operations to Beni and transitioning from the Unit’s narrow intelligence focus to a broader and more advanced electronic warfare signal intelligence capacity. The Unit’s structure included multiple intelligence detachments supported by outsourced hardware and software, costing about $829,848 monthly under an agreement active from 2021 to 2024. The Committee was further informed that the initial Headquarters Committee on Contracts review and endorsement was in 2018, the letter of assist was signed in November 2020 and services commenced in May 2021, with extensions approved in 2023 to 2024. However, restoring the same construct is not intended, as future developments must follow the re-establishment of key enablers such as intelligence, surveillance and reconnaissance, unmanned aircraft system assets and sufficient troop strength, as intelligence alone lacks operational impact. Taking into consideration the extended timelines for the sourcing of signal intelligence capability, as well as the need for related key enablers to be in place for its effective use, the Advisory Committee is of the view that the full deployment and operationalization is likely to experience delays, and recommends that a reduction of 10 per cent, or $1,000,000, be applied for related resources proposed under communications and information technology. The Committee trusts that updated information on the planned deployment timelines will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report.
Petrol, oil and lubricants

56.	A total provision of $22,477,784 is proposed for fuel, oil and lubricants for the 2026/27 period, reflecting a decrease of $2,254,616, or 9.1 per cent, primarily as a result of lower fuel costs under air operations ($9,272,449, a decrease of $2,160,751, or 18.9 per cent) and ground transportation ($3,646,021, a decrease of $1,118,379, or 23.5 per cent), partially offset by higher fuel costs under facilities and infrastructure ($9,559,314, an increase of $1,024,514, or 12.0 per cent). With regard to the ceasefire implementation mandate, additional provisions for fuel, oil and lubricants were included under facilities and infrastructure in the amount of $2,800,000 and ground transportation in the amount of $1,000,000. The Advisory Committee trusts that updated information on fuel costs will be provided to the General Assembly at the time of its consideration of the present report. The Committee further discusses fuel costs and management in its report on cross-cutting issues related to peacekeeping operations (A/80/604).
57.	Subject to its recommendations in paragraphs 43, 45, 48, 52 and 55 above, the Advisory Committee recommends approval of the proposals of the Secretary-General for operational costs.
Management of sea containers

58.	Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that the Mission currently holds 3,733 containers – 675 in stock, 2,448 in active use, 536 with troop- and police-contributing country units, 60 under write-off and 14 in transit – of which 36.5 per cent are under 5 years old, 45.2 per cent between 5 and 15 years and 18.2 per cent over 15 years. The Committee discusses the management of sea containers in its report on cross-cutting issues related to peacekeeping operations (A/80/604).
Prevention of sexual exploitation and abuse

59.	Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that to combat sexual exploitation and abuse, MONUSCO applies the United Nations three-pronged approach of prevention, enforcement and victim assistance – providing regular training, risk assessments and community outreach; ensuring timely investigation and disciplinary measures in coordination with oversight bodies; and offering comprehensive medical, psychological, legal and educational support to victims. The Mission also collaborates closely with community focal points and the prevention of sexual exploitation and abuse coordinator in Kinshasa to strengthen prevention and accountability across the Democratic Republic of the Congo. The Advisory Committee trusts that MONUSCO will continue to pursue its efforts to prevent sexual exploitation and abuse, ensure the accountability of perpetrators and provide protection and support to the victims, and that information thereon will be provided in future reports. The Committee discusses the special measures for protection against sexual exploitation and abuse in its related report (A/80/691).
Equitable geographical representation and gender parity

60.	Upon enquiry, the Advisory Committee was informed that, as at 31 December 2025, MONUSCO comprised 1,657 staff at all levels. Among the 509 international staff, 92 nationalities were represented. Of the total 1,657 staff, 384 were female and 1,273 were male.

61.	The international MONUSCO staff were from the following regional groups: African States (282); Asia-Pacific States (75); Eastern European States (34); Latin American and Caribbean States (30); and Western European and other States (73); as well as from the United States of America (15).

62.	The Advisory Committee trusts that MONUSCO will further pursue its efforts to improve equitable geographical representation and gender parity. The Committee further discusses equitable geographical representation and gender parity in its report on cross-cutting issues related to peacekeeping operations (A/80/604).

63.	The actions to be taken by the General Assembly with regard to the financing of MONUSCO for the period from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025 are indicated in section IV of the budget performance report (A/80/621). The Advisory Committee notes the unencumbered balance of $14,884,100 for the period from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025, as well as other revenue amounting to $30,420,100 from investment revenue ($9,558,100), other/miscellaneous revenue ($2,543,000) and the cancellation of prior-period obligations ($18,319,000) for the period ended 30 June 2025. The Committee also notes the proposal of the Secretary-General to place those amounts in a reserve that would be used in the event that collections are insufficient to enable the full, efficient and effective implementation of mandates. The Committee considers that this is a policy matter to be decided by the General Assembly. The Committee makes further comments in its report on cross-cutting issues related to peacekeeping operations (A/80/604).

64.	The actions to be taken by the General Assembly with regard to the financing of the Mission for the period from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027 are indicated in section IV of the report of the Secretary-General on the proposed budget (A/80/621). Taking into account its observations and recommendations above, the Advisory Committee recommends that the proposed resources be reduced by $8,441,500 from $874,790,100 to $866,348,600. Accordingly, the Committee recommends that the General Assembly appropriate the amount of $866,348,600 for the maintenance of the Mission for the 12-month period from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027. A summary of the budgetary impact and the financial implications resulting from the recommendations of the Committee with regard to the financing of MONUSCO for the period from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027 is presented in the table below.

Budgetary impact and the financial implications resulting from the recommendations of the Advisory Committee for the United Nations Organization Stabilization Mission in the Democratic Republic of the Congo for the period from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027

table_content.text: Reduce rate for common staff costs from 90.1% to 85.8%
10% reduction on new unmanned aircraft systems
10% reduction on new Geo-Localized Threat Assessment Unit capability
